BledDest's blog

Educational Codeforces Round 147 — Editorial
By BledDest, 3 years ago,

1821A - Matching

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

1821A - Matching

In a positive integer, the first digit is from 11 to 99, and every next digit can be any. This allows us to implement the following combinatorial approach:

  • calculate the number of different values for the first digit, which is 00 if the first character of ss is 0, 11 if the first character of ss is any other digit, or 99 if the first character of ss is ?;
  • calculate the number of different values for each of the other digits, which is 11 if the corresponding character of ss is a digit, or 1010 if it is ?;
  • multiply all these values.
Solution (BledDest)

#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main()
{
	int t;
	cin >> t;
	for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
	{
		string s;
		cin >> s;
		int ans = 1;
		if(s[0] == '0') ans = 0;
		if(s[0] == '?') ans = 9;
		for(int j = 1; j < s.size(); j++)
			if(s[j] == '?')
				ans *= 10;
		cout << ans << endl;
	}
}

1821B - Sort the Subarray

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

1821B - Sort the Subarray

Let's find the leftmost and the rightmost position in which the arrays aa and a′a′ differ. Since only the elements in the chosen subsegment might change, the subarray we sorted should contain these two positions.

Let's start with the subarray from the leftmost "different" position to the rightmost one, and then expand it to get the longest subarray which meets the conditions. Suppose we want to expand it to the left. Let the current left border be LL; how to decide if we can make it L−1L−1 or less? If a′L<aL−1aL′<aL−1, then we cannot include L−1L−1 in the subarray we sort, since otherwise the order of these two elements would have changed. Otherwise, aL−1aL−1 is not greater than any element in the subarray we have chosen, so we can include it and reduce LL by 11. We do this until it's impossible to reduce LL further.

The same goes for the right border: we expand it to the right until we find an element which is less than the previous element, and we cannot include this element in the subarray.

Solution (BledDest)

#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main()
{
	int t;
	scanf("%d", &t);
	for(int i = 0; i < t; i++)
	{
		int n;
		scanf("%d", &n);
		vector<int> a(n);
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
			scanf("%d", &a[i]);
		vector<int> a1(n);
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
			scanf("%d", &a1[i]);
		int diffl = -1, diffr = -1;
		for(int j = 0; j < n; j++)
		{
			if(a[j] != a1[j])
			{
				diffr = j;
				if(diffl == -1)
					diffl = j;
			}
		}
		while(diffl > 0 && a1[diffl - 1] <= a1[diffl])
			diffl--;
		while(diffr < n - 1 && a1[diffr + 1] >= a1[diffr])
			diffr++;
		printf("%d %d\n", diffl + 1, diffr + 1);
	}
}

1821C - Tear It Apart

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

1821C - Tear It Apart

The resulting string looks like a single letter repeated a number of times. That sounds too vague. Let's fix the exact letter cc that will be left. Since the size of the alphabet is only 2626, we can afford that.

The letters now separate into letters cc and other letters. And all other letters can be treated as indistinguishable from each other. Let's make letter cc into a binary 11 and any other letter into a binary 00. Our goal is to remove all zeros from the string with the given operations.

First, notice that it doesn't help you to removes ones. If some operation contains both ones and zeros, then taking ones out of it doesn't make any zeros in it adjacent. At the same time, these ones can only help you separate adjacent zeros later.

Thus, we have some blocks of zeros, separated by the blocks of ones. We want to remove only zeros. Notice how these blocks can be solved completely independently of each other. If you solve block 11 in cnt1cnt1 operations, block 22 in cnt2cnt2 operations, ..., block kk in cntkcntk operations, then you can solve the entire string in max(cnt1,cnt2,…,cntk)max(cnt1,cnt2,…,cntk) operations. Since the blocks are separated by the blocks of ones, you can combine the first operations for all blocks into one big operation and so on.

The only thing left is to calculate the number of operations for a single block. Let it have length ll. Basically, in one operation, you can decrease its length to ⌊l2⌋⌊l2⌋. You can see that the longer the block, the greater answer it has. So you can find the longest block first, then calculate the answer for it. You can either use this iterative formula or notice that it's a logarithm of ll in base 22 and calculate that however you want.

To find the lengths of the blocks of zeros, you can use two pointers.

Overall complexity: O(|AL|⋅n)O(|AL|⋅n) per testcase.

This problem can also be solved in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) on an arbitrarily large alphabet. Basically, when you fix a letter, you can tell the lengths of the blocks of other letters by looking at the occurrences of the letter. For occurrences i1,i2,…,iki1,i2,…,ik, the lengths of the blocks are i1−1,i2−i1,…,ik−ik−1,n−iki1−1,i2−i1,…,ik−ik−1,n−ik. So we can calculate the answer for a letter in O(number of its occurrences)O(number of its occurrences). The total of that for all letters is O(n)O(n).

Solution (awoo)

for _ in range(int(input())):
	s = input()
	n = len(s)
	ans = n
	for x in range(26):
		c = chr(x + ord('a'))
		i = 0
		cur = 0
		while i < n:
			j = i
			while j < n and (s[j] == c) == (s[i] == c):
				j += 1
			if s[i] != c:
				cur = max(cur, j - i)
			i = j
		if cur == 0:
			ans = 0
			break
		pw = 0
		while (1 << pw) <= cur:
			pw += 1
		ans = min(ans, pw)
	print(ans)

1821D - Black Cells

Idea: adedalic

Tutorial

1821D - Black Cells

Let's look at what's happening in the task: in the end the pointer will move into some position pp and some segments on the prefix [0,p][0,p] will be colored. Note that it's optimal to stop only inside some segment (or li≤p≤rili≤p≤ri for some ii), and if we colored xx segments (including the last segment [li,p][li,p] that may be colored partially) the answer will be equal to p+2⋅xp+2⋅x.

Let's prove that it's not optimal to skip segments with length len=r[i]−l[i]+1≥2len=r[i]−l[i]+1≥2. By contradiction, suppose the optimal answer aa has a skipped segment [li,ri][li,ri]. If we color that segment instead, we will make 22 more moves for pressing and releasing Shift, but we can make at least lenlen right move less. So the new answer a′=a+2−len≤aa′=a+2−len≤a — the contradiction.

Now we are ready to write a solution. Let's iterate over ii — the last segment we will color (and therefore the segment where we stop). At first, let's imagine we colored the whole segment [li,ri][li,ri] as well. Let ss be the total length of all segments on prefix [0,ri][0,ri] that are longer than 11 and cc be the number of segments of length 11 on this prefix.

There are three cases:

  • if s+c<ks+c<k, stopping inside the ii-th segment is not enough;
  • if s<ks<k but s+c≥ks+c≥k, we will color all "long" segments plus several short ones. The current answer will be equal to ri+2((i−c)+(k−s))ri+2((i−c)+(k−s)), where riri is where we stop, (i−c)(i−c) is the number of long segments and (k−s)(k−s) is the number of short segments we need;
  • if s≥ks≥k, then we don't need any short segments. More over, we can stop even before reaching riri. So, the current answer will be equal to ri−(s−k)+2(i−c)ri−(s−k)+2(i−c), where ri−(s−k)ri−(s−k) is the exact position to stop to get exactly kk black cells and (i−c)(i−c) is the number of long segments.
Note that ii is 11-indexed in calculations above, and we can stop the first moment we met the situation s≥ks≥k.

The answer is the minimum among the answers we've got in the process. Since it's easy to update values ss and cc when we move from ii to i+1i+1, the total complexity is O(n)O(n).

Solution (adedalic)

fun main() {
    repeat(readln().toInt()) {
        val (n, k) = readln().split(' ').map { it.toInt() }
        val l = readln().split(' ').map { it.toInt() }
        val r = readln().split(' ').map { it.toInt() }

        var ans = 2e9.toInt()
        var cntShort = 0
        var lenLong = 0
        for (i in 0 until n) {
            val curLen = r[i] - l[i] + 1
            if(curLen > 1)
                lenLong += curLen
            else
                cntShort++

            if (lenLong < k) {
                if (lenLong + cntShort >= k) {
                    val cntSegs = (i + 1 - cntShort) + (k - lenLong)
                    ans = minOf(ans, r[i] + 2 * cntSegs)
                }
            }
            else {
                ans = minOf(ans, r[i] - (lenLong - k) + 2 * (i + 1 - cntShort))
                break
            }
        }
        if (ans == 2e9.toInt())
            ans = -1
        println(ans)
    }
}

1821E - Rearrange Brackets

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

1821E - Rearrange Brackets

First, let's define the cost of an RBS a bit clearer. The absolute smallest cost of removing each pair of brackets is the number of bracket pairs it's inside of. That can actually be achieved — just remove the pairs right to left (according to the positions of the opening brackets in pairs). So you can instead say that the total cost is the sum of balance values after all closing brackets. Or before all opening brackets — these are actually the same.

From that, we can code a classic dp. Imagine we are not moving brackets, but instead doing that in two separate movements: put a bracket in some buffer and place it in the string. We'd love to use dp[pos][open][close][moves]dp[pos][open][close][moves] — the smallest answer if we processed pospos brackets, openopen opening brackets are in the buffer, closeclose closing brackets in the buffer and movesmoves are performed. Sadly, that doesn't really allow moving brackets to the left, since you would have to first place the bracket, then put in it the buffer. Does that actually break anything? Apparently, no. You can make these buffer states from −k−k to kk, and think of negative values as taking a loan. These states are enough to determine the current balance of the string. Thus, enough to both update the states and check if the string stops being an RBS after placing a closing bracket.

Overall complexity: O(nk3)O(nk3).

We can do it faster, but our proof isn't that convincing.

Start by showing that there exists an optimal answer such that each move leaves the sequence an RBS. Consider a sequence of moves that ends up being an RBS. First, you can basically rearrange the moves (maybe adjusting the exact positions is required). Second, there exists a move that, performed first, leaves an RBS. Make it and propagate the proof. You can show that such a move exists by studying some cases.

Then I found it more intuitive to switch to another representation — you can look at the forest induced by the bracket sequence. The roots of the trees in it are the topmost opening and closing brackets of the RBS. Their children are the inner topmost brackets for each of them, and so on. With that representation, the answer is actually the sum of depths of all vertices.

Now for the moves. Let's move an opening bracket to the right. We won't move it after its corresponding closing bracket to not break an RBS. How will it change the tree? It will turn some children of the corresponding vertex into the children of its parent. Thus, it will decrease their depths by one, and the depths of their descendants as well. How about to the left? That will turn some children of its parent into its own children, increasing their depths (and the depths of their descendants) by one. Similar analysis can be performed for the closing brackets.

The claim is that, in the optimal answer, you should only move opening brackets and only to the right. Then they decrease the answer independently of each other. It's pretty clear that the best position to move each bracket to is as much to the right as possible — place it next to its respective closing bracket. That will decrease the answer by the size of the subtree (excluding the vertex itself).

Finally, we want to choose kk vertices that have the largest sum of their subtrees. That can be just done greedily — pick kk largest ones.

You don't have to build the tree explicitly for that — the size of the subtree is half of the number of brackets between an opening bracket and a corresponding closing one. So, everything can be processed with a stack.

Overall complexity: O(n)O(n) or O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Solution 1 (awoo)

#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;
 
#define forn(i, n) for(int i = 0; i < int(n); i++) 

const long long INF64 = 1e18;

long long dp[2][11][11][6];

int main(){
	int t;
	cin >> t;
	while (t--){
		int k;
		cin >> k;
		string s;
		cin >> s;
		int n = s.size();
		forn(balo, 2 * k + 1) forn(balc, 2 * k + 1) forn(mv, k + 1) dp[0][balo][balc][mv] = INF64;
		dp[0][k][k][0] = 0;
		int act = 0;
		forn(ii, n){
			int i = ii & 1;
			int ni = i ^ 1;
			forn(balo, 2 * k + 1) forn(balc, 2 * k + 1) forn(mv, k + 1)
				dp[ni][balo][balc][mv] = INF64;
			forn(mv, k + 1) forn(balo, 2 * k + 1) forn(balc, 2 * k + 1) if (dp[i][balo][balc][mv] != INF64){
				int bal = act - balo + balc;
				if (balo >= 0 && mv < k)
					dp[i][balo - 1][balc][mv + 1] = min(dp[i][balo - 1][balc][mv + 1], dp[i][balo][balc][mv] + bal);
				if (balc >= 0 && mv < k && bal > 0)
					dp[i][balo][balc - 1][mv + 1] = min(dp[i][balo][balc - 1][mv + 1], dp[i][balo][balc][mv]);
				if (s[ii] == '('){
					dp[ni][balo][balc][mv] = min(dp[ni][balo][balc][mv], dp[i][balo][balc][mv] + bal);
					if (balo + 1 <= 2 * k)
						dp[ni][balo + 1][balc][mv] = min(dp[ni][balo + 1][balc][mv], dp[i][balo][balc][mv]);
				}
				else{
					if (bal > 0)
						dp[ni][balo][balc][mv] = min(dp[ni][balo][balc][mv], dp[i][balo][balc][mv]);
					if (balc + 1 <= 2 * k)
						dp[ni][balo][balc + 1][mv] = min(dp[ni][balo][balc + 1][mv], dp[i][balo][balc][mv]);
				}
			}
			act += (s[ii] == '(' ? 1 : -1);
		}
		printf("%lld\n", *min_element(dp[n & 1][k][k], dp[n & 1][k][k] + k + 1));
	}
}
Solution 2 (awoo)

for _ in range(int(input())):
	k = int(input())
	s = input()
	n = len(s)
	st = []
	cnt = [0 for i in range(n + 1)]
	ans = 0
	for i in range(n):
		if s[i] == '(':
			ans += len(st)
			st.append(i)
		else:
			cnt[(i - st.pop()) // 2] += 1
	for i in range(n, -1, -1):
		t = min(k, cnt[i])
		ans -= t * i
		k -= t
	print(ans)

1821F - Timber

Idea: BledDest

Tutorial

1821F - Timber

People say that this problem can be bashed with generating functions. Sadly, I know nothing about them, so I'll explain the more adhoc solution I managed to come up.

Let's investigate the general form of some placement of trees. Imagine we fixed some position x1,x2,…,xmx1,x2,…,xm for them. How to check if it's possible to cut them all down? Well, it's a simple greedy problem. Process trees left to right. If a tree can fall down to the left, make it fall to the left. Otherwise, if it can fall down to the right, make it fall to the right. Otherwise, no answer.

Try a harder problem. Consider the spots the fallen trees take. How many constructions can these spots be induced by after the greedy algorithm is performed? First, these taken spots are actually segments of length k+1k+1. Each tree can fall either from the left or the right side of the segment. To determine the answer, one has to look at the number of free spots to the left of each fallen tree. If there're at least kk free spots, then the tree can only fall from the left side of that segment. Otherwise, it can fall both ways. Thus, for xx trees that have less than kk free spots to the left of them, the answer is 2x2x.

We can't really iterate over the constructions, so let's try yet another related problem. Would be cool if we could fix the exact number of trees that have more than kk free cells to the left of them. I don't really know how to do that. But I know how to fix at least the amount. Let that be at least xx trees. For xx trees, make their segments of length 2k+12k+1 — k+1k+1 for the tree itself and kk extra cells to the left of it. For the rest m−xm−x trees, make their segments of length k+1k+1. Then we have to place them on the given nn spots. The path is as follows. Rearrange the long and the short segments among each other — (mx)(mx). Then use stars and bars to choose the amount of space between the segments — (n−x⋅(2k+1)−(m−x)⋅(k+1)+mm)(n−x⋅(2k+1)−(m−x)⋅(k+1)+mm). Finally, multiply by 2m−x2m−x to fix the side for the short segments.

Initially, we thought that we could just use PIE to calculate the exact answer from that — ∑x=0mf(x)⋅(−1)x∑x=0mf(x)⋅(−1)x. And the results of this formula coincided with the naive solution, so we thought that everything should be fine. But unfortunately, even though the formula is right, using PIE in the described way is incorrect. Let us show you the correct way.

Let F(x)F(x) be the number of ways to choose the segments for the trees in such a way that xx fixed segments are "long" (i. e. at least of length 2k+12k+1). To calculate F(x)F(x), we use the familiar stars and bars concept: (n−x⋅(2k+1)−(m−x)⋅(k+1)+mm)(n−x⋅(2k+1)−(m−x)⋅(k+1)+mm) (we already had this formula earlier).

Now, let G(x)G(x) be the number of ways to choose the segments for the trees in such a way that xx fixed segments are "long", and all other segments are "short". The formula for G(x)G(x) is a straightforward application of PIE: G(x)=∑y=0m−x(−1)y(m−xy)F(x+y)G(x)=∑y=0m−x(−1)y(m−xy)F(x+y), where we iterate on yy — the number of segments that should be short, but are definitely long.

The answer to the problem can be calculated as ∑x=0m2m−x(mx)G(x)∑x=0m2m−x(mx)G(x) — we choose which xx segments are "long", and all others should be "short". Expanding G(x)G(x) gives us the following formula:

∑x=0m∑y=0m−x(−1)y2m−x(mx)(m−xy)F(x+y)∑x=0m∑y=0m−x(−1)y2m−x(mx)(m−xy)F(x+y)

which, after expanding binomial coefficients into factorials and getting rid of (m−x)!(m−x)!, becomes

∑x=0m∑y=0m−x(−1)y2m−xm!x!y!(m−x−y)!F(x+y)∑x=0m∑y=0m−x(−1)y2m−xm!x!y!(m−x−y)!F(x+y)

We introduce the substitution variable z=x+yz=x+y, and the formula becomes

∑z=0m∑y=0z(−1)y2m−z+ym!(z−y)!y!(m−z)!F(z)∑z=0m∑y=0z(−1)y2m−z+ym!(z−y)!y!(m−z)!F(z)

By multiplying both the numerator and the denominator by z!z!, we then get

∑z=0m∑y=0z(−1)y2m−z+y(mz)(zy)F(z)∑z=0m∑y=0z(−1)y2m−z+y(mz)(zy)F(z)

∑z=0m(mz)2m−zF(z)∑y=0z(−2)y(zy)F(z)∑z=0m(mz)2m−zF(z)∑y=0z(−2)y(zy)F(z)

And ∑y=0z(−2)y(zy)∑y=0z(−2)y(zy) is just (1−2)z(1−2)z. Thus, the answer is equal to ∑z=0m(−1)z(mz)2m−zF(z)∑z=0m(−1)z(mz)2m−zF(z).

Overall complexity: O(n)O(n).

Solution (awoo)

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;

#define forn(i, n) for(int i = 0; i < int(n); i++) 

const int MOD = 998244353;

int add(int a, int b){
	a += b;
	if (a >= MOD)
		a -= MOD;
	if (a < 0)
		a += MOD;
	return a;
}

int mul(int a, int b){
	return a * 1ll * b % MOD;
}

int binpow(int a, int b){
	int res = 1;
	while (b){
		if (b & 1)
			res = mul(res, a);
		a = mul(a, a);
		b >>= 1;
	}
	return res;
}

int main(){
	int n, m, k;
	scanf("%d%d%d", &n, &m, &k);
	
	vector<int> fact(2 * n + 1), rfact(2 * n + 1);
	fact[0] = 1;
	for (int i = 1; i <= 2 * n; ++i) fact[i] = mul(fact[i - 1], i);
	rfact[2 * n] = binpow(fact[2 * n], MOD - 2);
	for (int i = 2 * n - 1; i >= 0; --i) rfact[i] = mul(rfact[i + 1], i + 1);
	auto cnk = [&](int n, int k){
		if (k < 0 || n < 0 || k > n) return 0;
		return mul(fact[n], mul(rfact[k], rfact[n - k]));
	};
	auto snb = [&](int n, int k){
		return cnk(n + k, k);
	};
	
	int pw2 = 1;
	int ans = 0;
	for (int i = m; i >= 0; --i){
		int cur = 0;
		if (n - (m - i) * 1ll * (k + 1) - i * 1ll * (2 * k + 1) >= 0)
			cur = mul(snb(n - (m - i) * (k + 1) - i * (2 * k + 1), m), mul(pw2, cnk(m, i)));
		ans = add(ans, i & 1 ? -cur : cur);
		pw2 = mul(pw2, 2);
	}
	
	printf("%d\n", ans);
}
Tutorial of Educational Codeforces Round 147 (Rated for Div. 2)
  • +78
  • BledDest
  • 3 years ago
  • 43

+8

Initially, we thought that we could just use PIE to calculate the exact answer from that — ∑mx=0f(x)⋅(−1)x∑x=0mf(x)⋅(−1)x. And the results of this formula coincided with the naive solution, so we thought that everything should be fine. But unfortunately, even though the formula is right, using PIE in the described way is incorrect.

That is precisely how I thought of doing PIE for this problem as well, so I'm surprised to hear it's wrong. Why is that?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    psoet
    3 years ago, show (+5) #
    »
    »
    psoet
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    I think that's because the coefficient 2m−x2m−x remains the same (xx is the exact size of the set), but the size of a subset you emurate during PIE doesn't.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      smax
      3 years ago, show (+4) #
      »
      »
      »
      smax
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +10

      I don't think the coefficient being constant is necessarily an issue, there are plenty of applications of PIE which have fixed coefficients based on subset size such as counting derangements:

      ∑nx=0(−1)x(nx)(n−x)!∑x=0n(−1)x(nx)(n−x)!

      According to this reference, PIE can be used to count "exact" constraints based on "at least" constraints:

      N=∅=∑T⊆E(−1)|T|N⊇TN=∅=∑T⊆E(−1)|T|N⊇T

      In this case, the property is that a segment is long instead of short, and the formula counts exactly none of the segments being long in terms of subsets of segments such that at least these segments are long, so under these lens the simple PIE solution seems valid.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        psoet
        3 years ago, show (+3) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        psoet
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 3 →  
        0

        The example you mentioned differed from this situation. The coefficients used in counting derangements are just the number of certain permutations.

        The PIE can be paraphrased as: given 2 functions f,gf,g on sets that satisfy

        f(S)=∑S⊆Tg(T)f(S)=∑S⊆Tg(T)

        Then

        g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|

        In this problem, the ff we calculated is not the sum of 2m−|T|2m−|T| over all possible T, but the number of Ts multiplied by the same 2m−|S|2m−|S|. So the formula isn't valid here.

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          smax
          3 years ago, show (+2) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          smax
          3 years ago, hide # ^ |
          ← Rev. 2 →  
          +3

          Ok, so tell me if I'm understanding this correctly.

          The f(x)f(x) used in the "wrong" solution is

          f(x)=(mx)(n−x(2k+1)−(m−x)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−xf(x)=(mx)(n−x(2k+1)−(m−x)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−x

          Now go to the subset interpretation of PIE, if we define ff and gg as:

          • f(S)f(S) is the number of ways where at least the intervals in SS are long
          • g(S)g(S) is the number of ways where exactly the intervals in SS are long

          then

          f(T)=(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|f(T)=(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|

          as this is just the f(x)f(x) mentioned in the editorial (without the (mx)(mx) because we're only considering one subset and not all subsets of size xx), and

          g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|=∑S⊆T(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|⋅(−1)|T|−|S|g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|=∑S⊆T(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|⋅(−1)|T|−|S|

          and therefore our answer is

          g(∅)=∑T(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|⋅(−1)|T|=∑x=0m(mx)(n−x(2k+1)−(m−x)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−x⋅(−1)xg(∅)=∑T(n−|T|(2k+1)−(m−|T|)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−|T|⋅(−1)|T|=∑x=0m(mx)(n−x(2k+1)−(m−x)(k+1)+mm)⋅2m−x⋅(−1)x

          The coefficient used here is 2m−|T|2m−|T|, not 2m−|S|2m−|S|. And this reasoning seems identical to the reasoning used for the "wrong" solution, which is expressing gg as an alternating sum of ff.

            I think it may be because when you say

            g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|g(S)=∑S⊆Tf(T)(−1)|T|−|S|

            it should be

            g(S)=∑S⊆T(m—|S||T|—|S|)f(T)(−1)|T|−|S|g(S)=∑S⊆T(m—|S||T|—|S|)f(T)(−1)|T|−|S|

            because you need to choose which of the current short segments are long segments in the terms of the PIE.

            Interestingly, they give the same result though.

A few typos in F editorial, based on my understanding:

"If there're at least k free spots, then the tree can only fall from the left side of that segment." "left" should be right.

"For x trees, make their segments of length 2k+1-k+1 for the tree itself and k extra cells to the left of it." "2k+1-k+1" should just be k+1.

In the formula two lines above "Overall complexity", there are two F(z)F(z). One of them should be deleted.

Hopefully this clears things up for people reading the editorial.

Also, here is a problem utilizing Inclusion-Exlusion that is very similar to the subproblem in F:

link

+6

Here is my thoughts about how to solve F: let aiai be the number of configurations of fallen logs such that exactly ii logs have a gap of kk empty space to their left. We want to compute ∑i=0m2m−i⋅ai∑i=0m2m−i⋅ai. But, the only information we have right now is a separate array bb such that bi=∑j=0m(ji)⋅ajbi=∑j=0m(ji)⋅aj. If you imagine arranging all the coefficients of aa in bibi in a matrix where Mi,jMi,j is the coefficient of ajaj in bibi, then the matrix is an upper triangular matrix which looks like pascal's triangle. We want to do something kind of like gaussian elimination on the matrix in order to determine which coefficients on the rows yield the vector [1,12,14,...][1,12,14,...] when you add the rows up to get one horizontal vector. The correct coefficients (and I just extrapolated this after trying the first few columns by hand) are [1,−12,14,−18,...][1,−12,14,−18,...]. Then you can try to prove the general fact that ∑k=0n(nk)(−2)−k=2−n∑k=0n(nk)(−2)−k=2−n. I think this is more intuitive than magically rearranging the long expression in the editorial so that a known identity shows up.

+6

I was confused regarding the proof for problem E's claim that there exists an optimal answer such that each moves results in an RBSRBS. (Note that the condition of the problem is that only the resulting sequence after all kk moves should be an RBSRBS, no condition about the intermediate sequences).

So, I managed to come up with a decent proof. First of all, we know in one move we remove some bracket and place it somewhere. We can freely first choose to remove all kk brackets then start placing these brackets in their required positions. Clearly, both are equivalent. Consider an optimal sequence of moves in which —

Say, we have removed (( at positions: i1,i2,i3..i1,i2,i3.. and we placed some (( at positions j1,j2,j3..j1,j2,j3..

We can map these i′si′s with j′sj′s arbitrarily. Suppose we map (i2,j3)(i2,j3) then we can consider bracket at position i2i2 "moving" at position j3j3. We can call a pair of mapping a move also. Call a pair of mapping (ia,jb)(ia,jb) "good" if ia≤jbia≤jb.

Also, define this stuff similarly for )) type of brackets. (We again map their indexes from which they are removed to the indexes where they have been placed, however in this case call a pair of mapping (ka,wb)(ka,wb) "good" if wb≤kawb≤ka, here kaka is the index where bracket is removed and wbwb is the index where it is inserted back).

Now here comes the important part, if the sequence is an RBSRBS, then performing a "good" mapping pair over it will always result in the sequence which is an RBSRBS too. Also, once the sequence turns non−RBSnon−RBS then any "bad" mapping pair/move performed will never turn it to RBSRBS again. So, the kk pairs of mapping that we got, just sort them such that all "good" mappings are to the left of any "bad" mapping.

This will be the sequence of moves such that after every move the resulting sequence is an RBSRBS. Initially sequence is an RBSRBS, and goes through all the "good" pairs of mapping, the sequence is guaranteed to remain an RBSRBS, then it goes through "bad" pairs of mappings, now the sequence can't turn into non−RBSnon−RBS because, if it turns into non−RBSnon−RBS then it contradicts the fact that after all the kk moves have been performed the final sequence is an RBSRBS ! (There is no way the sequence can switch from non−RBSnon−RBS to RBSRBS which going through the pairs of "bad" mappings !)

→ Reply

I see fft tag for F, does anyone have any idea about it pls?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Sakuyalove
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Sakuyalove
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    My solution used fft. Consider mm segments with length k+1k+1, the length of the interval between the (i−1)th(i−1)th segment and the ithith segment([−k,0][−k,0] as the 0th0th segment) is lili. Hence we have ∑i=1mli≤n−(k+1)m∑i=1mli≤n−(k+1)m and li≥0li≥0 for i=1,2,...,mi=1,2,...,m.

    Now we will determine whether each tree is at the left or right endpoint of the segment. To avoid repetition, we claim that for each tree it must fall down to the left unless it can't fall down to the left side. So the tree of the ithith segment can be left endpoint only if li<kli<k. And in any case a tree can always be a right endpoint. So the answer is ∑l1+...+lm≤n−(k+1)m2∑i=1m[li<k]∑l1+...+lm≤n−(k+1)m2∑i=1m[li<k].

    Now consider f(x)=2(1+x+...+xk−1)+(xk+xk+1+...)=11−x+1−xk1−x=2−xk1−xf(x)=2(1+x+...+xk−1)+(xk+xk+1+...)=11−x+1−xk1−x=2−xk1−x, the answer is ∑i=0n−(k+1)m[xi]fm(x)∑i=0n−(k+1)m[xi]fm(x), where fm(x)=(2−xk)m(1−x)−mfm(x)=(2−xk)m(1−x)−m, we have (2−xk)m=∑i=0m(mi)(−1)i2m−ixki(2−xk)m=∑i=0m(mi)(−1)i2m−ixki,(1−x)−m=∑i=0∞(m+i−1m−1)xi(1−x)−m=∑i=0∞(m+i−1m−1)xi, so use fft, we can get fm(x)fm(x), and then we can get answer. This solution is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

    → Reply

while( l>=1 && b[l]>=b[l-1])l--; while(r<(n-1) &&b[r]<=b[r+1] )r++;

cout<<l+1<<" "<<r+1;

Just checking the longest segment in array a` which is increasing and not equal to the segment in a ..and using stl like pair<int,pair<int,int>> = {-1,{-1,-1}} and using max on it to store the segment with largest length and not equal to the segment in a. ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

define ll long long

define f(i, n) for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)

define f1(i, n) for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)

define f11(i, n) for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)

define fr(i, n) for (int i = n — 1; i >= 0; i--)

const int MOD = 1e9 + 7;

void solve() { int n; cin >> n; vector a(n + 1), b(n + 1); f(i, n) cin >> a[i]; f(i, n) cin >> b[i]; b.push_back(-1); a.push_back(-1); ll ans = -1; pair<int, pair<int, int>> p = {-1, {-1, -1}}; f(i, n) { int last = b[i]; int count = 1; int start = i + 1; vector x, y;

while ((i < n) && (b[i + 1] >= b[i]))
    {
        x.push_back(a[i]);
        y.push_back(b[i]);
        count++;
        i++;
    }
    int end = i + 1;
    if (x != y)
    {
        p = max(p, {count, {start, end}});
    }
}
cout << p.second.first << " " << p.second.second;
cout << endl;

}

int main() { ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(NULL); int t; cin >> t; while (t--) solve(); return 0; } ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

→ Reply
